content.text: Extended Thinking is a native Claude capability that enables a structured internal reasoning phase before producing a final response. Rather than generating an answer immediately, Claude in extended thinking mode works through the problem systematically — decomposing it, considering multiple approaches, evaluating trade-offs, catching errors in its own logic, and synthesizing a conclusion — before writing the visible reply. The result is noticeably better answers on hard problems that require careful, multi-step reasoning.
What Is Extended Thinking

Extended Thinking is a feature available through the Claude API and select interfaces that lets Claude allocate additional computation to an internal monologue before generating the user-visible answer. This internal monologue — sometimes called a thinking block — is a series of reasoning steps that Claude works through silently. It is different from asking Claude to 'think step by step' in a prompt, because extended thinking is a native model behavior that the model has been specifically trained to use effectively.

When Claude is in extended thinking mode, its processing happens in two distinct phases. In the first phase, the thinking phase, Claude reasons through the problem without worrying about how the reasoning looks to the user. It can explore dead ends, backtrack, reconsider assumptions, do rough calculations, and weigh competing hypotheses. In the second phase, the response phase, Claude synthesizes its internal reasoning into a clear, user-facing answer. The final answer reflects all the work done in the thinking phase but is written cleanly, without the exploratory messiness of the internal reasoning.

Extended thinking is available for the most capable Claude models. It is controlled at the API level through a thinking parameter in the request body. Developers can set a budget, measured in tokens, for how much internal reasoning Claude is allowed to do. A larger budget means more thorough reasoning but higher cost and latency. The feature can also be surfaced in some end-user-facing tools, though it is primarily a developer-facing capability.

It is important to understand what extended thinking is not. It is not simply more text generation. The internal reasoning phase is processed differently from the final answer — it uses a different computational mode that allows more flexible, exploratory cognition. Think of it as the difference between scratch paper work and a polished write-up: the scratch paper can be messy and exploratory; the write-up is clear and conclusive.
Why Extended Thinking Matters

Standard Claude responses are already high quality for most tasks. But a subset of tasks — particularly those requiring deep logical reasoning, multi-step inference, careful planning, or self-correction — benefit significantly from extended thinking. For these tasks, giving Claude 'thinking room' can meaningfully improve accuracy, consistency, and depth.
Higher accuracy on complex reasoning tasks: Extended thinking reduces errors on problems requiring chains of logic, such as multi-step math, code debugging, and legal analysis.
Better handling of ambiguous problems: Claude can reason through multiple interpretations and pick the most defensible one rather than defaulting to the most obvious reading.
Improved self-consistency: By working through a plan before responding, Claude is less likely to contradict itself within a long or detailed answer.
Effective self-correction: During the thinking phase, Claude can catch errors in its own initial reasoning and correct them before they appear in the output.
Deeper exploration of trade-offs: For design and architecture questions, Claude can evaluate multiple options against criteria and explain its reasoning for the recommendation.
More reliable for high-stakes tasks: In domains where an incorrect answer has real consequences — medical reasoning, legal analysis, financial modeling — extended thinking adds analytical rigor.
Better complex code generation: When writing intricate algorithms or designing data structures, Claude can reason through the logic completely before writing any code.
Reduced hallucination on hard problems: Extended thinking gives Claude opportunity to notice when it is uncertain and hedge appropriately rather than guessing confidently.
How Extended Thinking Works
The Thinking Budget

The thinking budget is a configurable parameter that controls how many tokens Claude can spend on internal reasoning. It is set in the API request and acts as an upper limit. Claude will use up to the budget if the problem warrants it, but will stop thinking early if it reaches a satisfactory conclusion sooner. A budget of 5,000 to 16,000 tokens is typical for challenging tasks. Some very complex problems may benefit from even larger budgets.
Internal Monologue Structure

Within the thinking phase, Claude's internal reasoning is structured but flexible. It begins by restating and decomposing the problem — identifying what is being asked, what information is available, and what sub-problems need to be solved. Then it works through each sub-problem, drawing on its knowledge and making inferences. Throughout, it checks its own work — does this conclusion follow from the premises? Is this calculation correct? Does this plan have any gaps? Finally, it synthesizes the sub-conclusions into a unified answer.
Response Generation After Thinking

Once the thinking phase is complete, Claude generates the final response. This response is a clean, user-facing answer that reflects the conclusions from the thinking phase. The response is written in the model's normal conversational or instructional style, without any of the exploratory messiness of the internal reasoning. The two phases are clearly separated — users see only the final response unless the developer chooses to surface the thinking block as well.
When to Use Extended Thinking
Complex mathematical problems: proofs, multi-step calculations, optimization problems, and combinatorial reasoning.
Intricate code debugging: finding subtle logic errors in concurrent code, tracing complex execution flows, or reasoning about memory management.
System design and architecture: evaluating multiple design patterns and their trade-offs for a given set of requirements.
Detailed legal or regulatory analysis: reasoning about how a specific rule applies to a specific fact pattern, including exceptions and edge cases.
Strategic planning and decision-making: analyzing options against multiple criteria and recommending the best course of action with full reasoning.
Academic and research analysis: evaluating the validity of arguments, identifying methodological weaknesses, and synthesizing findings from multiple sources.
Complex medical reasoning: working through a differential diagnosis or evaluating treatment options against patient-specific contraindications.
Financial modeling: structuring a valuation model, reasoning through assumptions, and stress-testing conclusions.
Step-by-Step Guide to Using Extended Thinking

Get API access to a supported model: Extended thinking is currently available for Claude Opus models. Ensure you have an Anthropic API key and are using an SDK that supports the thinking parameter. Python, TypeScript, and direct HTTP are all supported.

Add the thinking parameter to your request: In your API call, set the thinking parameter to an object with type 'enabled' and a budget_tokens value. For example in Python: thinking={'type': 'enabled', 'budget_tokens': 10000}.

Set an appropriate minimum token budget: Anthropic requires a minimum thinking budget. Check the documentation for the current minimum, but 1,024 tokens is typical. Too small a budget may not provide any benefit over standard generation.

Write a focused, specific prompt: Extended thinking produces the best results when your prompt clearly defines the problem, provides all relevant context, and specifies what a good answer looks like. Vague prompts lead to unfocused thinking.

Submit the request and handle the response: The API response will include a thinking block if thinking was performed. The main answer will be in the text content block as usual. Your code may need to handle the additional content blocks in the response.

Optionally surface the thinking: If you want to show the reasoning to users, extract the thinking block content from the response and display it alongside the answer. Use this feature carefully — raw thinking content can be verbose and exploratory.

Benchmark and tune the budget: Compare answer quality with different budget sizes for your specific task type. Find the minimum budget that produces acceptable quality, balancing cost and latency against accuracy.
Practical Examples
Example 1: Mathematical Proof Assistance

A student asks Claude to prove that the square root of 2 is irrational using proof by contradiction. Without extended thinking, Claude might state the proof correctly but skip justification steps. With extended thinking, Claude works through each logical step in the thinking phase — setting up the contradiction assumption, deriving the implications, identifying where the contradiction arises, and verifying that the logic is airtight — before presenting a complete, rigorously justified proof.
Example 2: Architecture Design Review

A software team shares a proposed microservices architecture and asks Claude to evaluate it for scalability and resilience. Extended thinking lets Claude examine each service boundary, trace the data flows, identify potential single points of failure, reason about the performance characteristics under load, consider how service failures would propagate, and then formulate a comprehensive review with prioritized recommendations. The thinking phase allows this multi-dimensional analysis to happen coherently.
Example 3: Complex Code Debugging

A developer shares a multi-threaded Python application that has a race condition causing intermittent failures. Claude uses extended thinking to trace the execution paths across threads, identify the critical section where shared state is accessed without proper synchronization, reason about the specific interleaving of thread schedules that triggers the bug, verify that a proposed lock strategy would fix the issue without introducing deadlock, and then present a clear diagnosis and fix.
Example 4: Legal Analysis

A compliance officer asks Claude whether a specific business practice violates a regulation. Extended thinking allows Claude to parse the relevant regulatory text carefully, identify the elements that the regulation requires, map those elements to the specific facts of the business practice, consider how regulators have interpreted similar provisions, reason about potential defenses or safe harbors, and reach a well-reasoned conclusion — rather than giving a generic hedged response.
Configuration and Integration
Thinking Budget Guidelines

The right thinking budget depends on the complexity of the task. For moderately complex problems, 4,000 to 8,000 tokens is usually sufficient. For highly complex problems — intricate code, deep analysis, complex proofs — budgets of 16,000 to 32,000 tokens may be warranted. Beyond 32,000 tokens, additional budget often yields diminishing returns. Start with a moderate budget and increase it only if you can demonstrate measurable quality improvement.
Cost and Latency Management

Extended thinking tokens are billed at the same rate as other tokens in most configurations. The thinking budget adds directly to the total token cost and to the time required to generate a response. For latency-sensitive applications, extended thinking is appropriate only when response time is not critical. For batch processing, cost analysis, and research workflows, the cost is often justified by the quality improvement.
Combining with Other Features

Extended thinking works alongside other Claude capabilities. It can be combined with tool use, allowing Claude to reason through when and how to use tools before invoking them. It works with system prompts, allowing you to configure Claude's behavior and then apply extended thinking on top of that configuration. It is compatible with streaming, allowing partial results to be returned as Claude generates them.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Using extended thinking for simple tasks: It adds latency and cost without benefit for tasks like summarization, formatting, or simple factual lookup. Reserve it for genuinely complex reasoning.
Setting the budget too small: A budget of 100 to 200 tokens may produce no benefit — Claude cannot reason meaningfully in so few tokens. Use at least 1,000 and typically 5,000 or more.

Vague prompts with complex thinking: Extended thinking amplifies both the quality and the direction of Claude's reasoning. A vague prompt leads to unfocused thinking. Specificity helps Claude think productively.
Ignoring the thinking output: If you surface the thinking block, take time to review it. It can reveal misunderstandings about the problem that indicate prompt improvement opportunities.

Not comparing with standard generation: Always benchmark extended thinking against standard responses for your task to verify that it produces measurable improvement before deploying it in production.

Applying it universally in a mixed-use application: In an application where users ask both simple and complex questions, apply extended thinking selectively to queries that have been classified as complex.

Forgetting that thinking is exploratory: The thinking block contains tentative, exploratory reasoning. Do not interpret uncertain statements in the thinking block as Claude's final position on a topic.
Limitations and Trade-offs
Higher latency: The thinking phase adds seconds to response time, which is unacceptable for real-time conversational applications but fine for analytical workflows.
Higher cost: More tokens are consumed per request, increasing API spend. For high-volume applications, the cost increase must be justified by the quality benefit.
Model availability: Extended thinking is only available on select top-tier Claude models, not all Claude model variants.
Not available in all interfaces: The Claude.ai web app and most third-party integrations do not expose extended thinking as a user-facing option.
Cannot overcome knowledge gaps: Extended thinking improves reasoning over what Claude knows, but it cannot produce knowledge that Claude does not have from its training.
Thinking quality varies by task type: For some problem types, the benefit of extended thinking is marginal. Empirical testing with your specific task is necessary to determine whether it helps.
Tips and Best Practices
Profile your task mix before enabling extended thinking: Identify which 10 to 20 percent of your queries are complex enough to benefit, and apply it selectively.
Use extended thinking in design and planning phases: When Claude is helping plan a project, design a system, or draft a complex document, extended thinking adds the most value.
Treat thinking budgets like performance budgets: set a target and stick to it, only increasing it when you have data showing the need.
Log and review thinking blocks during development: Reading Claude's internal reasoning helps you understand how it approaches your tasks and identifies opportunities to improve your prompts.
Combine with a clear output format specification: After the thinking phase, Claude should know exactly what format the answer should take. Specify this clearly to get consistently structured outputs.
For interactive products, cache results of extended thinking on deterministic inputs: Avoid re-running expensive reasoning on the same problem repeatedly.
Document when and why you use extended thinking in your codebase: Other developers who work on the system should know which calls use this feature and why.
Test with and without extended thinking in parallel for a set of representative queries: Use this data to build a classifier that decides whether a given query needs extended thinking.
Frequently Asked Questions
How does extended thinking differ from chain-of-thought prompting?

Chain-of-thought prompting adds instructions to the prompt asking Claude to show its reasoning — phrases like 'think step by step' or 'explain your reasoning before answering'. Extended thinking is a native model-level feature where Claude uses a dedicated reasoning mode before generating the response. The key difference is that extended thinking uses a qualitatively different computational process, not just a different prompt instruction. Extended thinking is generally more reliable and produces higher-quality reasoning than prompt-based chain-of-thought, especially on hard problems.
Can I hide the thinking block from users?

Yes. By default, the thinking block is returned as a separate content block in the API response, and you choose whether to display it to users. Most production applications do not surface the raw thinking block to users, since it contains exploratory and tentative reasoning that may be confusing. The thinking block is primarily useful for developers debugging Claude's behavior or applications where transparent reasoning is part of the product experience.
Does extended thinking always produce better answers?

No. Extended thinking is most beneficial for tasks that genuinely require careful multi-step reasoning. For straightforward tasks — summarization, simple question answering, creative writing, formatting — it adds cost and latency without meaningfully improving the output. The benefit also varies between problem types; empirical testing for your specific use case is necessary to determine whether extended thinking is worthwhile. Think of it as a targeted tool rather than a universal upgrade.
Is extended thinking available outside the API?

As of mid-2025, extended thinking is primarily a developer API feature. Anthropic and some third-party tools may surface extended thinking in consumer-facing products, but the most complete and controllable access is through the API. Check the Anthropic documentation and Claude.ai release notes for the latest information about where extended thinking is available in user-facing products.
In-Depth Analysis

Extended Thinking deserves careful analysis because it touches on fundamental aspects of how Claude works and how you can most effectively leverage it. This section explores the topic from multiple angles to give you a complete understanding.

From a theoretical standpoint, understanding the underlying principles helps you predict behavior in novel situations. From a practical standpoint, knowing how to apply these principles effectively is what enables you to build reliable, high-quality systems.
Theoretical foundation: Why does this work the way it does?
Practical application: How do I apply this in real projects?
Edge cases: What unusual situations should I be aware of?
Integration: How does this interact with other Claude features?
Evolution: How has this changed and where is it going?
Implementation Patterns
There are several well-established implementation patterns for Extended Thinking that have emerged from real-world usage. These patterns capture best practices and help you avoid common pitfalls.

Pattern 1 — Start Simple: Begin with the most straightforward implementation. Don't add complexity until you understand the simple version completely. This pattern applies universally and prevents premature optimization.
Pattern 2 — Fail Fast: Detect errors early and surface them clearly. When something goes wrong, the earlier you catch it, the easier it is to diagnose and fix.
Pattern 3 — Iterate Based on Data: Collect metrics from real usage and use them to guide improvements. Intuition is a starting point, but data reveals the truth.
Document patterns as you discover them to build organizational knowledge
Share patterns with your team to avoid duplicate work
Test patterns thoroughly before adopting them as standard practice
Be willing to abandon patterns that don't work for your specific context
Optimization Techniques
Once you have a working implementation of Extended Thinking, there are several optimization techniques that can improve performance, reduce costs, or enhance the user experience.

Cost optimization is often the first priority, especially for applications with high request volumes. The key strategies include using the most efficient model for each task, minimizing context window usage, and leveraging caching where appropriate.
Performance optimization focuses on reducing latency and improving throughput. Streaming responses, parallel requests, and efficient prompt design all contribute to better performance.
Profile before optimizing — measure actual bottlenecks, not assumed ones
Optimize for the constraints that matter most to your users
Test optimizations thoroughly to ensure they don't compromise quality
Document optimizations so future maintainers understand why they exist
Monitor optimization impacts over time to ensure they continue to deliver value
Quality Assurance

Maintaining quality when working with Extended Thinking requires systematic testing and monitoring. Quality in AI applications is different from traditional software because outputs are probabilistic rather than deterministic.

Set up evaluation frameworks that can test your implementation against representative examples. Track quality metrics over time and investigate regressions promptly. Use both automated testing and human review for a comprehensive quality assurance program.
Define clear quality criteria before building — what does 'good' look like?
Create a test dataset that represents real-world usage patterns
Run evaluations regularly, not just during development
Track quality metrics alongside cost and performance metrics
Involve end users in quality assessment through feedback mechanisms
Practical Application Guide

Understanding the practical application of this feature in real-world scenarios is essential for making the most of it. This guide provides concrete guidance for integrating this capability into your projects effectively.

Start by identifying the specific scenarios in your application where this capability adds the most value. Build a focused proof of concept to validate your understanding, then expand gradually as you gain confidence. Track the impact on your application's quality and user experience to guide further development.
Define clear acceptance criteria before implementation to guide your development
Build incrementally and test each stage before moving to the next
Collect user feedback early and often to ensure you're solving real problems
Monitor usage patterns to identify opportunities for optimization
Document what you learn for future team members working on the same feature
Plan for graceful degradation in case the capability is unavailable or unreliable
Set up proper error handling and fallback behaviors from the start
Consider the UX implications of capability limitations when designing your system
Integration with Your Existing Stack

Integrating this Claude capability with your existing technology stack requires thoughtful planning. The integration points depend on your architecture, but there are general patterns that apply across different tech stacks.

For web applications, the integration typically involves server-side API calls that keep your API keys secure. For backend services, you might integrate directly into your data processing pipelines. Mobile applications typically communicate through a backend proxy to avoid exposing credentials.
Never expose Anthropic API keys in client-side code or version control
Use environment variables or secrets management for all credentials
Implement proper rate limiting to stay within your API tier limits
Add request logging to help debug issues and optimize usage
Consider implementing a queue for high-volume applications
Test your integration thoroughly in a staging environment before production
Summary

This section provided a comprehensive overview of this advanced capability, covering both the theoretical foundations and practical implementation guidance. Apply these insights to build more powerful and effective Claude-powered applications.
